meta.title: 1.4 User Segments and Their Failure Modes
meta.description: Master the failure modes of Make's three user segments — developers who exceed the abstraction, non-technical builders who can't recover from errors, and AI agents who amplify platform weaknesses — and why all three require separate UI surfaces over one shared runtime.

blocks.prompt:
> Figma Make serves three user types: developers, non-technical builders, and AI agents. How does each group break the platform differently?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module explains why serving developers, non-technical builders, and AI agents simultaneously is an infrastructure architecture problem, not a UX problem. By the end, you'll be able to describe each segment's failure mode and recovery path, explain why the three segments require separate product surfaces over a shared runtime, and apply the concept of abstraction gradients to evaluate platform design decisions.

Developers, non-technical builders, and AI agents each break Make's platform in structurally different ways — requiring not one product surface but three abstraction layers (canvas, code view, API) sharing a single underlying runtime, which is the defining architectural challenge of the Platform PM role. Make's three segments have incompatible mental models, incompatible failure modes, and incompatible recovery strategies. The platform must serve all three — or explicitly choose which to prioritize and accept the consequences.

**Segment 1: Developers**

Developers using Make are experienced engineers who understand the code being generated. They use Make for speed — to accelerate scaffolding, to prototype ideas, to generate boilerplate — not because they cannot write the code themselves.

**What they want:**
- Framework opinions they can live with (or override)
- Code they can read, understand, and modify without fighting the generator
- Escape hatches: the ability to drop down to raw code when the abstraction fails
- Integration with their existing toolchain: Git (version control system), CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery — automated pipelines that test and deploy code), their preferred IDE
- Deployment targets they control (AWS, GCP, their own Kubernetes cluster — the container orchestration platform)

**How they break the platform:**
Developers exceed the abstraction. They want to add a custom webpack plugin (a build tool extension), configure ESLint rules (a code quality linter), use a specific version of a dependency, or restructure the project in a way Make's generator doesn't expect. When they do this, the generated code and the manual modifications conflict. The platform's model of the project diverges from the actual project state.

This is the "synchronization problem" — Make generates code based on a model of the project; the developer modifies the project outside that model. On the next generation, Make may overwrite the developer's changes, or generate code that conflicts with the modified state.

The recovery path for developers: they can read the code, understand the conflict, and resolve it manually. The cost is friction and lost time.

**The abstraction gradient for developers:** They want visible code, editable configuration, CLI (Command Line Interface — a text-based tool for running commands directly) access, and integration depth. The platform must expose these without making them required for simpler users.

**Segment 2: Non-Technical Builders**

Non-technical builders are designers, PMs, marketers, and domain experts who want software without the software engineering. They are not learning to code — they are trying to build a product. The code is a means to an end, not a thing of value in itself.

**What they want:**
- Natural language interaction throughout (not just at prompt time)
- Visual verification: I can see it works without reading code
- Automatic error recovery: if something breaks, fix it without asking me to understand why
- Simple deployment: one click, done, it's live
- No maintenance burden: the platform keeps it running

**How they break the platform:**
Non-technical builders hit errors they cannot interpret. "Module not found: Can't resolve '@/components/Button'" is meaningless to someone who has never seen a module system (the system by which code files import functionality from each other). The error message is developer-oriented; the user is not a developer. They cannot debug, cannot read the code, cannot tell the agent what specifically went wrong.

The failure mode is abandonment. Non-technical users who hit an unrecoverable error do not file bug reports — they leave. The platform's quality bar must be much higher for this segment because the recovery path is much narrower.

A secondary failure mode: non-technical users generate apps that work but are not secure. They deploy without reviewing for hardcoded secrets. They ship apps with no error handling because the demo worked. The platform has a safety responsibility to this segment that it does not have for developers (who can evaluate risk themselves).

**The abstraction gradient for non-technical builders:** They want no code visible, natural language everything, automatic error handling, and guardrails. The platform must hide complexity without pretending it doesn't exist.

blocks.question: Non-technical builders are described as the segment with the 'narrowest recovery path.' What does this mean in practice, and why does it imply Make needs a higher quality bar for this segment than for developers?
blocks.answer: Recovery path refers to what a user can do when something breaks. A developer who sees a broken preview can read the error, understand the stack trace, edit the code, and fix it. A non-technical builder who sees a broken preview has no equivalent option — they can retry the prompt, contact support, or abandon. This asymmetry means the platform must prevent more failures for non-technical users (because they can't fix them) and surface failures in natural language if they do occur. A 5% broken-preview rate is annoying for developers; it's catastrophic for non-technical users who will simply churn. This is why Make's quality investment has to be weighted toward the segment with the narrowest recovery path — not just the largest segment.

blocks.content:
**Segment 3: AI Agents**

This is the emerging segment — other AI systems using Make's platform as infrastructure. Make's Canvas-Direct AI Agents capability (April 2026) means autonomous agents can generate, modify, and deploy applications without human prompting per step. The agent is the user.

**What they need:**
- Stable, programmatic APIs (not a UI)
- Deterministic error responses (machine-readable, not human-readable)
- Rate limits that accommodate agent request patterns (bursty, high-volume)
- Execution sandboxes that tolerate unusual code patterns
- Webhooks and callbacks for async results (agents don't wait at a UI)

**How they break the platform:**
Agents generate edge-case code at scale. An agent exploring the solution space may generate 50 variations of a component, each subtly different, to find the optimal one. It may call Make's APIs in patterns that look like abuse from a rate-limiting perspective. It may generate code that is syntactically valid but semantically unusual — patterns the preview infrastructure has never seen.

Agents also fail at the tail of the distribution. A human user prompts Make 5–10 times per session. An agent may prompt 500 times. The 1% of prompts that produce broken previews become 5 prompts for a human user (annoying but tolerable) and 5 failures per agent session (a systematic problem).

**The abstraction gradient for agents:** They want API-first access, machine-readable responses, high rate limits, and programmatic deployment. They do not want a UI.

## How to Think About This

The three segments sit at opposite ends of an abstraction dial. Developers want the dial turned down — expose the code, expose the config, expose the errors. Non-technical builders want the dial turned up — hide the code, translate errors to plain English, make every action one click. Agents don't want a dial at all — they want a clean API with machine-readable responses. A single product surface cannot satisfy all three. The platform's solution is not to pick one setting; it is to build separate layers that share the same runtime, so the output is identical regardless of which surface generated it.

**Why Serving All Three Simultaneously is the Hardest Problem**

The abstraction gradients for each segment point in different directions:
- Developers want less abstraction (more control)
- Non-technical builders want more abstraction (less complexity)
- Agents want no UI at all (just APIs)

A single UI cannot satisfy all three. The platform must architect multiple surfaces:
- A visual canvas for non-technical builders (maximum abstraction)
- A code view and CLI for developers (minimum abstraction)
- A REST/GraphQL API (a standard web API format) for agents (no UI)

And these surfaces must share the same underlying runtime, the same preview infrastructure, and the same deployment pipeline. The complexity of this multi-surface architecture is why Make's Platform PM role is not a feature PM role — it is an infrastructure PM role.

**The Figma Analogy**

Figma itself has this three-segment problem:
- Professional designers want every tool, every plugin, every precision control
- Non-designers want templates and AI magic that produces good output without training
- Developers want accurate design tokens, Dev Mode, and API access

Figma's answer: layers of abstraction with escape hatches. Non-designers use templates and AI; professionals use components and auto-layout; developers use Dev Mode and the REST API. The same underlying system, multiple surfaces.

Make must build the same layered abstraction — and the Platform PM must design the layers before the engineering team implements them.

---

blocks.question: A non-technical user's deployed Make app is returning 500 errors in production because an environment variable was not set on the deployment platform. The user cannot read the logs, cannot identify the missing variable, and cannot fix it without help. Walk through how the platform should handle this — at each layer.
blocks.answer:
Layer 1 — Prevention: The platform should detect required environment variables at build/deploy time and prompt the user to set them before deployment completes. Layer 2 — Detection: The platform should monitor deployed apps for error rates and alert the user in natural language ("Your app is returning errors — here's what we think is wrong"). Layer 3 — Recovery: The platform should offer a one-click "diagnose and fix" flow that identifies the missing variable, prompts the user to supply a value, and redeploys. Developers would find the logs and fix it manually. Non-technical users need all three layers to have any recovery path.

---

blocks.title:
Take a Make (or Lovable/Bolt) generated app and deliberately introduce a runtime error — remove a required prop, delete an import, introduce a typo in an API URL. Then observe what the platform shows the user. Is the error message interpretable by a non-technical user? What recovery options does the platform offer? Grade the error experience for each of the three segments.

---
blocks.solution: After introducing the error, observe the error display carefully and grade it against each segment. For developers: the platform should show a stack trace or at minimum the file name and line number where the error occurred — this is information a developer can act on. Does it? For non-technical builders: the error message should be in plain English (not "TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'map')" but something like "This part of the app couldn't load its data — try refreshing or ask Make to fix it"). Is it? For agents: if you were an agent calling the preview API, would the error response be machine-readable JSON with an error code and structured description, or a human-readable string? In most current implementations you'll find: developers get a reasonable error (often readable); non-technical users get the same developer-oriented error with no translation; agents get whatever the UI renders (not structured). Score each: 1 (unusable), 2 (partially usable), 3 (usable). Most platforms score 3/1/1 — usable for developers, not for the other two. This exercise makes the abstraction gradient concrete: the gap between 'shows the error' and 'surfaces it in a way each segment can act on' is exactly the platform investment the module is arguing for.

blocks.prompt:
Explain to a Figma VP of Product why the decision to "serve developers and non-technical builders" is actually a more complex platform architecture decision than it sounds — using the concept of abstraction gradients.

---

questions: Think of a platform or internal tool you built or used that had to serve multiple user segments with different technical depth — maybe ML engineers and product managers, or power users and casual users. How did the abstraction gradient problem manifest, and how did the team resolve it (or not)?

When you've managed or worked on AI-powered infrastructure — whether a model API, an automation pipeline, or an AI-assisted tool — how did AI systems as consumers of that infrastructure differ from human users in the ways they broke things? What guardrails or architecture changes did that drive?

---

blocks.content:
Developers, non-technical builders, and AI agents each break Make's platform in structurally different ways — requiring not one product surface but three abstraction layers (canvas, code view, API) sharing a single underlying runtime, which is the defining architectural challenge of the Platform PM role.

---

blocks.prompt:
> Every segment's needs connect back to one question: what does Figma uniquely have that makes Make worth building here rather than as a standalone tool? That's Make's competitive position — and it's next.

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: What is the 'synchronization problem' that developers cause when using Make, and which other segment cannot cause this same failure mode?
options: Developers working in different time zones cause synchronization problems when they edit the same component simultaneously

Developers modify project files outside Make's model of the project state; on the next generation Make may overwrite those changes or produce conflicting code — a failure only possible for users who can access and edit the underlying code
Developers cause synchronization problems by switching between multiple Make projects without saving, corrupting the session state
reviewQuestions.explanation: The synchronization problem occurs when a developer manually edits generated code — restructuring files, changing configuration, modifying components — while Make's internal project model remains unchanged. When Make generates next, it may overwrite those manual changes or produce code that conflicts with the new state. Non-technical builders can't cause this failure because they can't access or modify the underlying code. They have different failure modes: hitting errors they cannot interpret or deploy.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the synchronization problem is exclusive to users with code access, which is why segment-specific failure analysis shapes platform architecture.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: This isn't about time zones or session management. The synchronization problem is specific to developers who can edit generated code directly. When their manual changes diverge from Make's internal project model, the next generation may conflict with or overwrite their work. Non-technical users can't cause this because they can't touch the code — their failure modes are entirely different.

reviewQuestions.question: Why do AI agents as a user segment require a higher platform reliability target than human users?
options: An agent may issue 500 requests per session versus a human's 5–10 — so a 1% failure rate that produces a handful of broken previews for a human user becomes systematic failures across every agent session
Agents require higher reliability because they run in production environments while humans only use Make for prototyping
Agents are more sensitive to latency than humans and require sub-10ms response times to function correctly
reviewQuestions.explanation: The amplification dynamic is the key insight about agents as a segment. A human making 10 requests per session with a 1% failure rate experiences roughly 1 broken preview per 10 sessions — annoying but tolerable. An agent making 500 requests per session with the same 1% failure rate experiences 5 systematic failures per session — a reliability crisis. Platform weaknesses that are acceptable for human users become catastrophic at agent request volumes.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — the amplification effect is what makes agents an architecturally distinct segment, not just faster users.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The reliability requirement isn't about latency thresholds or production vs. prototyping contexts. It's about amplification: agents issue many more requests per session than humans, so any given failure rate produces far more absolute failures per session. A 1% failure rate that human users barely notice becomes a systematic problem for agents. That's why the reliability target must be higher.

reviewQuestions.question: The module proposes three separate product surfaces for Make's three user segments — a visual canvas, a code view/CLI, and a REST API. Why must all three share the same underlying runtime?
options: A shared runtime is required for compliance with data privacy regulations that mandate all user data be processed in one location
Sharing a runtime reduces infrastructure costs because Figma only needs to maintain one execution environment instead of three

All three surfaces must produce identical behavior from the same generated code — if each surface ran a different execution environment, the same app could behave differently depending on which surface created it
reviewQuestions.explanation: Behavioral consistency is the reason for shared runtime. If a developer's code-view edits, a non-technical builder's canvas edits, and an agent's API calls all converged on different execution environments, the same app might behave differently depending on which surface was used. The shared runtime guarantees that output is consistent regardless of which surface generated it. The challenge is building three radically different interface layers (maximum abstraction, minimum abstraction, no UI) on top of that single consistent execution core.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good — consistent behavior across surfaces is the correctness argument, and it's what makes this an infrastructure PM challenge rather than a UX challenge.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Cost and compliance are secondary concerns. The core reason is behavioral correctness: if canvas, code view, and API ran on different runtimes, the same project could produce different results depending on which surface was used. Shared runtime ensures consistency. The hard part is building three opposite-abstraction surfaces (hide everything / expose everything / no UI at all) on top of one consistent execution environment.
